sn17.5:1.2: “Monks, gains, offerings, & fame are a cruel thing, a harsh, bitter obstacle to the attainment of the unexcelled rest from bondage.
sn17.5:1.3: Suppose there were a beetle, a dung-eater, full of dung, gorged with dung, with a huge pile of dung in front of him.
sn17.5:1.4: He, because of that, would look down on other beetles:
sn17.5:1.5: ‘Yes, sirree! I am a dung-eater, full of dung, gorged with dung, with a huge pile of dung in front of me!’
sn17.5:1.6: In the same way, there is the case where a certain monk—conquered by gains, offerings, & fame, his mind consumed—adjusts his lower robe and, taking his bowl & outer robe, goes into a village or town for alms.
sn17.5:1.7: Having eaten there as much as he likes—full of almsfood & invited again for the next day—
sn17.5:1.8: he goes to the monastery and, in the midst of a group of monks, boasts,
sn17.5:1.9: ‘I have eaten as much as I like, I am full of almsfood & have been invited again for tomorrow. I am a recipient of robes, almsfood, lodgings, & medicinal requisites for curing illness. These other monks, though, have next to no merit, next to no influence. They aren’t recipients of robes, almsfood, lodgings, & medicinal requisites for curing illness.’
sn17.5:1.10: Conquered by gains, offerings, & fame, his mind consumed, he looks down on other well-behaved monks.
sn17.5:1.11: That will be for this worthless man’s long-term suffering & harm.
sn17.5:1.12: That’s how cruel gains, offerings, & fame are: a harsh, bitter obstacle to the attainment of the unexcelled rest from bondage. “So you should train yourselves: ‘We will put aside any gains, offerings, & fame that have arisen; and we will not let any gains, offerings, & fame that have arisen keep our minds consumed.’
sn17.5:1.13: That’s how you should train yourselves.”
